patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old woman. Be vague and a bit evasive; answer briefly unless the clinician asks specific questions. Start by saying you keep getting short of breath and tired, and your mom says you snore loud and sometimes stop breathing. Volunteer: constant tiredness even after a full night, loud snoring, waking up gasping, winded after one flight of stairs, some morning headaches and dry mouth, broad thumbs/big toes and “different-looking” fingers.

Only if asked: mom notices breathing pauses, daytime sleepiness/dozing off, weight gain, nighttime heartburn, constipation, recurrent ear infections/hearing issues, learning slower/needed school help.

Start with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: constant bathroom trips even without recent drinking, drinking water all day due to dry mouth, waking multiple times nightly to pee, and feeling wiped out/foggy from poor sleep.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as 'idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension' and links symptoms to elevated pulmonary vascular resistance and pulmonary arterial pressures.
Explains that PAH can cause 'right heart strain' or right ventricular dysfunction/failure due to increased afterload on the right ventricle.
Uses clear, patient-appropriate language and checks understanding (e.g., asks the patient to summarize or invites questions).
Mentions key red-flag symptoms that warrant urgent evaluation (e.g., syncope, worsening dyspnea at rest, chest pain, signs of right heart failure).
Does not prescribe medications in an L1 task; focuses on diagnosis documentation and appropriate education/next steps.
If discussing future treatment, includes the concepts 'pulmonary vasodilators' and 'oxygen' as potential therapies (without initiating prescriptions), aligned with the communication milestones.
Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., suggesting OTC decongestants/stimulants for dyspnea, or advising strenuous exercise without individualized guidance).
If any medication is contemplated hypothetically, explicitly notes that allergy, drug interaction, and contraindication checks must be completed before prescribing (safety checks acknowledged).
